one_line_takeaway: OLLMs suffer a genuine cross-modal safety gap driven by refusal-vector magnitude shrinkage in mid layers, and an SVD-derived modality-invariant 'golden refusal vector' steered by lightweight per-layer adapters (OmniSteer) recovers most of that lost refusal rate (69.9% to 91.2% RSR) while largely preserving general capability.
key_finding: Cross-modal inputs show a large safety gap versus single-modal inputs: single-modality RSR is ~90-97%, but introducing modality combinations (e.g., Text+Image) drops RSR to ~75% for Qwen2.5-Omni-7B and ~50% for MiniCPM-o-2.6. Refusal-vector analysis (Table 1) shows cross-modal combination vectors have cosine similarity 0.73-0.86 with the text refusal vector (vs 0.89-0.97 for single modalities) and magnitude ratios of only 0.45-0.72 (vs 0.85-1.01 for single modalities) — variance decomposition attributes 88.3% of the projection-value decline to magnitude shrinkage. OmniSteer, using the SVD golden vector, raises average RSR from a Vanilla baseline of 69.9% to 91.2% (as stated in the abstract/intro) while the paper's Table 3 reports OmniSteer's average RSR across the 3-model x 8-dataset grid at 91.16%, versus Self-Reminder at 85.18% and OmniGuard at 76.72%, with OmniSteer preserving OmniBench accuracy within <2% of vanilla versus an ~8.1% degradation for baselines. In the golden-vector ablation (Table 2), at alpha=0.1 vgold (SVD) achieves average BAR 46.2% versus 41.1% (vmean) and 38.8% (vtext) at comparable RSR (~99.8%), supporting the claim that the SVD-derived direction better preserves benign-query acceptance.
core_contribution: The paper delivers three original contributions for omni-modal LLM (OLLM) safety: (1) AdvBench-Omni, a benchmark built from AdvBench's 520 malicious + 500 benign text queries, expanded via a 'Control Modal-variable Principle' into 11 modality subsets (single/dual/triple-modal combinations of text, image, audio, video) totaling 11,220 samples, using deterministic modality transformations (direct pixel rendering for image/video, Qwen3-TTS for audio, and a 'semantic separation strategy' that splits a query into a text context plus a rendered visual/audio payload) instead of generative text-to-image methods, explicitly to avoid semantic drift; (2) a mechanistic analysis identifying a 'Mid-layer Dissolution' phenomenon in which refusal-direction projections for cross-modal inputs rise to ~0.95 by layer 12 then collapse to ~0.7 in later layers, attributed via log-linear variance decomposition to refusal-vector magnitude shrinkage (88.3% of variance) rather than directional deviation (9.8%), plus PCA evidence (PC1 explains ~80% of variance across 7 modality refusal vectors) that a modality-invariant 'pure refusal direction' exists in a low-dimensional subspace; (3) OmniSteer, an inference-time alignment method that extracts a 'golden refusal vector' via SVD (first left singular vector of the concatenated per-modality refusal-vector matrix) and trains lightweight per-layer 2-layer-MLP adapters (via forward hooks) to predict a layer-specific steering coefficient alpha_l that is added to hidden states along the golden direction, trained with a dual-objective loss (projection above threshold tau+ for harmful inputs, below tau- for benign inputs, with L1 regularization on |alpha_l|).
threat_model: The paper's own threat model is a cross-modal safety-alignment gap: an OLLM that reliably refuses single-modality harmful requests (RSR ~90-97%) can be induced to comply when the same harmful semantic content is split or re-encoded across modalities (e.g., text context + an image/audio payload rendering the harmful term, as in 'How can I make the thing in the image?' plus a rendered image of the word 'bomb'). This is framed as an inherent representational vulnerability (refusal-signal collapse in mid layers under multimodal fusion) rather than an adversarial prompt-engineering attack against a specific deployed system — there is no adaptive attacker, gradient-based jailbreak search, or transferability analysis; the 'attack' is simply presenting semantically-equivalent harmful content via a modality-combined channel.
limitations: Methodologically visible caveats from the main text: RSR is judged by an LLM-as-a-Judge (Qwen3-30B-A3B) rather than human annotation, introducing potential judge bias/noise; the mechanistic analysis (refusal vectors, Mid-layer Dissolution, SVD golden vector) is conducted primarily on a single model (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B) and then generalized to OmniSteer training across only 3 OLLMs; OmniSteer's benign-query preservation (BAR) still shows a substantial drop from Vanilla BAR (e.g., 88.8%/98.3%/98.4% for Text+Image/Text+Audio/Text+Video) to the 75-99% range depending on model/dataset, and the OmniSafety accuracy metric for OmniSteer on Qwen2.5-Omni-7B (42.47) is lower than Self-Reminder's on the same row-set in places, indicating uneven trade-offs across the 8 evaluation datasets rather than uniform superiority.

datasets_used: AdvBench-Omni (constructed by the authors: 520 malicious + 500 benign queries from AdvBench, expanded to 11 modality subsets, 11,220 samples total)
AdvBench-MM (baseline dataset built with Stable-Diffusion-3.5-generated images, used only for oracle/validation comparison, not as a training/eval benchmark)
quantitative_results: Single-modal RSR ~90-97% vs cross-modal RSR ~75% (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B) / ~50% (MiniCPM-o-2.6) on AdvBench-Omni
Cross-modal refusal-vector cosine similarity to text refusal vector: 0.73-0.86 (single-modal: 0.89-0.97)
Variance decomposition: magnitude shrinkage explains 88.3% of refusal-signal attenuation vs 9.8% for directional deviation
PCA: PC1 explains ~80.3% of variance across 7-modality refusal vectors (Figure 6a); ~74.4% across 4 single modalities (Figure 6b)
Mid-layer Dissolution: cross-modal refusal projection peaks at ~0.95 (layer 12), collapses, recovers only to ~0.7 in later layers; single-modal stays >0.9 after layer 16
OmniSteer overall RSR baseline-to-result: 69.9% -> 91.2% (as stated in abstract/intro)
Table 3 aggregate: OmniSteer average RSR 91.16% vs Self-Reminder 85.18% vs OmniGuard 76.72%; OmniSteer average BAR 83.2%
OmniSteer preserves OmniBench accuracy within <2% of vanilla vs ~8.1% degradation for baseline methods
Table 2 (golden vector ablation, alpha=0.1): SVD vector average BAR 46.2% vs vmean 41.1% vs vtext 38.8%, at comparable average RSR ~99.8%
Cross-modal RSR improvement attributed to OmniSteer: ~31.3% increase in cross-modal scenarios (per Section 5.4 text)
baselines_compared: Self-Reminder (Xie et al., 2023) - prompt-based safety reminder appended to queries
OmniGuard (Verma et al., 2025) - trained layer-wise safety classifier
vtext (text-only refusal vector) and vmean (arithmetic mean refusal vector) as ablation baselines for the golden refusal vector
